responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark faces a core tension between immediate resilience through trusted platform ecosystems and longer-term sovereignty through supplier diversification. The plan must move beyond privacy rhetoric to concrete procurement, standards, and governance actions that enable a credible fallback authentication path without disrupting service continuity. The primary levers are: (1) align procurement with platform-neutral and fallback-compatible requirements to reduce lock-in; (2) push EU-standard interoperability to normalize cross-border trust anchors; (3) demonstrate non-production fallback models to de-risk strategic choices; (4) build a cross-sector coalition to counter incumbent resistance and accelerate reform; (5) frame resilience and dependency risks to justify budget and timelines; (6) ensure regulatory and privacy compliance to maintain legitimacy; (7) establish a physical Copenhagen hub to coordinate work and demonstrations.

levers.name: Procurement language reform to mandate platform-neutral fallback paths
levers.consequences: Imposes platform-neutral criteria on future procurements and contracts, reducing vendor lock-in risk. Downstream, it may provoke longer negotiation cycles and require comprehensive standardization work across multiple stakeholders.
options: Institute mandatory platform-neutral fallback requirements in all future AltID procurements and related contracts.
Mandate a phased de-risking plan to replace platform-dependent signals with open, standards-based equivalents before procurement.
Implement a go/no-go gating process tied to independent interoperability testing across multiple vendors.
levers.review_lever: This lever governs the tension between platform-neutral procurement and incumbent supplier incentives, but the options overlook the transition costs and renegotiation frictions.

levers.name: EU-standard alignment and advocacy for platform-neutral wallet interoperability
levers.consequences: Strengthens Denmark's and the EU's interoperability posture by embedding platform-neutral requirements into policy and procurement. Downstream, it may trigger pushback from platform vendors and complicate negotiations where EU standards diverge from national practices.
options: Embed platform-neutrality requirements in EU Digital Identity Wallet policy submissions and national procurement guidelines.
Pilot cross-border interoperability tests with EU partners to validate AltID alignment and fallback pathways.
Lobby for harmonized NSIS-compliant fallback standards across member states to reduce vendor lock-in.
levers.review_lever: This lever frames EU interoperability versus national sovereignty, but the options underestimate the risk of conflicting standards across member states.

levers.name: Credible non-production demonstrators for platform-neutral fallback authentication
levers.consequences: Provides evidence that standards-based fallback is feasible without production-risk integration. Downstream, it requires careful scoping to avoid implying production readiness and may incur ongoing maintenance costs.
options: Develop a mobile demonstrator on GrapheneOS using WebAuthn and OpenID Connect to show standards-based authentication against a simulated identity provider.
Create a browser-based or hardware-token fallback demonstrator that operates independently from mobile OS integrity signals.
Publish open-source reference implementations and test vectors to encourage external validation and critical review.
levers.review_lever: This lever tests feasibility versus production readiness, but the options underestimate ongoing maintenance and certification requirements.

levers.name: Coalition governance and stakeholder engagement model
levers.consequences: Establishes formal governance to accelerate reforms, gains legitimacy with civil society, and coordinates messaging. Downstream, governance overhead may slow decision gates and create scope creep if disagreements persist.
options: Form a formal multi-stakeholder coalition with civil society, academia, and regional authorities to drive procurement reforms.
Establish a regulatory-watch group to monitor policy shifts and report blockers to decision gates.
Schedule regular policy rounds with digital governance committees to align expectations and timelines.
levers.review_lever: This lever aims to reduce policy fragility through coordinated advocacy, but the options ignore timing pressures and potential veto points in government processes.

levers.name: Resilience framing and dependency-risk analytics
levers.consequences: Frames risk as a policy argument to justify investment in platform-neutral paths and fallback capabilities. Downstream, it may invite polarized debate if data is perceived as sensational; it requires credible evidence.
options: Publish a resilience and continuity risk assessment focused on supplier concentration and single-point-of-failure risks.
Develop a transparent cost-of-dependency model and timeline for substituting platform-dependent components.
Secure contingency funding commitments from the budget authority to support fallback-capability development.
levers.review_lever: This lever reframes risk as resilience value, but the options may overstate certainty without robust data and funding commitments.

levers.name: Regulatory and privacy alignment with Danish/EU digital identity frameworks
levers.consequences: Aligns with privacy and regulatory expectations, reducing compliance risk and enabling smoother stakeholder engagement. Downstream, it could constrain design choices or introduce delays if regulators require extensive documentation.
options: Draft a regulatory impact assessment highlighting platform independence and privacy protections within AltID planning.
Propose amendments to procurement and data-access laws to require platform-neutral fallback options.
Engage the Data Protection Agency on cross-border identity data handling and interoperability requirements.
levers.review_lever: This lever aligns with privacy and compliance; options may slow innovation if regulatory cycles become lengthy.

levers.name: Copenhagen-based security research hub and coordination facility
levers.consequences: Provides a physical base for demonstrations, stakeholder meetings, and secure testing, supporting credibility and rapid iteration. Downstream, fixed location costs and security requirements add ongoing overhead and may limit flexibility.
options: Establish a Copenhagen-based security research hub with controlled access for demonstrations and stakeholder briefings.
Create a shared facilities agreement with a Danish university for secure testing and rapid iteration.
Implement a rotating on-site staff and contractor roster to maintain continuity and reduce reliance on a single expert.
levers.review_lever: This lever anchors physical coordination, but the options may underplay ongoing facility maintenance costs and security overhead.

responses.summary: Add 'platform-neutral procurement language for AltID-related components' to Procurement Strategy.

responses.strategic_rationale: Denmark’s digital identity position hinges on reducing dependency on foreign platform controls while preserving secure, verifiable access to public services. The core tensions balance speed of policy reform against continuity risk, cost and complexity of diversifying trust anchors, sovereignty aims with European interoperability, and the political economy of procurement changes. The levers jointly push for platform-neutral fallback pathways via procurement reform, credible open standards demonstrations, and EU-aligned governance, without assuming immediate production replacement of MitID. This combination creates a credible path to resilience and sovereignty by building evidence, standards leverage, and stakeholder consensus that can influence procurement language and procurement decisions.

levers.name: AltID platform-neutral fallback authentication procurement criteria
levers.consequences: Expanding procurement criteria to mandate platform-neutral fallback authentication increases resilience by reducing vendor lock-in and ensuring alternate trust paths exist. The downstream implication is longer procurement cycles and greater negotiation overhead with incumbents, but it creates a credible basis for continuity if platform policies change.
options: Mandate platform-neutral fallback authentication as a condition in AltID procurement, specifying open standards and external security attestations.
Establish a staged supplier-diversification clause that rewards collaborations with non-Apple/Google ecosystems through defined milestones.
Institute a sunset mechanism to phase out dependency on single platform vendors if fallback paths fail validation.
levers.review_lever: This lever targets the tension between platform dependence and supplier diversification, but the options miss explicit timelines and the scale of renegotiation effort. It also overlooks potential legal or contractual barriers in multi-vendor transitions.

levers.name: Non-production demonstrators for platform-neutral trust anchors
levers.consequences: Establishes credible evidence that alternative trust anchors can operate independently from production MitID, enabling policy and procurement justification without risking live services. This approach may invite scrutiny over the relevance of non-production results to real-world risk and could face pressure to accelerate certification timelines, potentially compromising rigor.
options: Deploy a mobile-focused demonstrator validating FIDO2/WebAuthn against simulated identity-provider environments using open standards.
Develop a browser-based fallback authentication prototype to decouple from mobile OS trust signals and hardware dependencies.
Coordinate a hardware-token-based demonstration to test portable credentials across multiple devices and browsers.
levers.review_lever: This lever addresses testing of alternative trust anchors in a non-production context, but the options miss a concrete plan for translating evidence into procurement decisions. It also lacks alignment with EU certification processes that would give credence to demonstrations across borders.

levers.name: Resilience-focused procurement governance and supplier diversification
levers.consequences: Shifts procurement away from single-vendor dependence toward diversified ecosystems, improving continuity and strategic autonomy. Downstream: may face resistance from incumbents and longer procurement cycles; could increase upfront complexity and governance overhead.
options: Institute mandatory diversification clauses that require onboarding multiple credential providers across different ecosystems.
Create cross-agency procurement standards that codify platform-neutral evaluation criteria and independent attestations.
Leverage master procurement agreements to pre-qualify vendors across regions to reduce lock-in.
levers.review_lever: This lever reframes procurement toward resilience and diversification, but the options miss concrete timelines and governance sequencing. They also risk budget stress if cross-agency standards proliferate.

levers.name: EU interoperability alignment with Danish wallet strategy
levers.consequences: Aligns domestic policy with European requirements, increasing legitimacy and potential funding; expands influence in standard-setting. Downstream: risk of diluting national preferences; slower decision-making due to multi-stakeholder consensus.
options: Engage EU bodies to harmonize platform-neutrality requirements with European Digital Identity Wallet specifications.
Publish a national guidance note mapping Danish AltID goals to NSIS and eIDAS2 frameworks for vendors and procurement teams.
Sponsor cross-border pilots with neighboring countries to test portable identity across ecosystems.
levers.review_lever: This lever advances European alignment, but the options overlook concrete timelines for national policy uptake. It may stall progress without a dedicated leadership sponsor.

levers.name: Continuity-focused service-level and continuity clauses in critical contracts
levers.consequences: Formalizes fallback authentication expectations during outages, enhancing resilience and public trust; prompts planning for service continuity and risk transfer. It may trigger higher governance overhead and complicate annual budgeting.
options: Incorporate continuity-of-service requirements into SLAs with agencies and critical providers.
Define audit and testing regimes that validate fallback authentication performance under contingency scenarios.
Establish a mandatory review cycle for AltID resilience provisions within national digital identity policy.
levers.review_lever: This lever embeds resilience in contracts, but the options miss explicit triggers for activation of fallback paths and measurement thresholds. It may create governance overhead that slows agile responses.

levers.name: Independent regulatory oversight for platform-neutral identity initiatives
levers.consequences: Creates an independent regulatory and security-review pathway to scrutinize platform-neutral identity initiatives, improving accountability and risk governance. Downstream: adds compliance overhead and may slow progress, requiring clear governance to avoid stalling.
options: Establish a periodic independent security review panel that evaluates platform-neutral fallback implementations against NSIS risk models.
Convene a Danish-regulatory task force to align policy signals with EU digital identity governance.
Publish quarterly public progress reports detailing risk, mitigations, and procurement updates.
levers.review_lever: This lever ensures oversight, but the options miss a fast-track path for time-sensitive demonstrations. It could burden small teams with compliance work.

responses.summary: Add a cross-agency platform-neutral identity pilot to AltID procurement governance lever.

responses.strategic_rationale: A concise strategic analysis of Denmark's digital identity initiative emphasizes reducing dependency on a narrow vendor base, embedding platform-neutral fallback paths, and aligning procurement, standards, and governance to withstand regulatory and political friction. The levers below challenge core assumptions about single-vendor dependence, accelerate credible evidence through non-production demonstrators, and broaden coalitions with EU bodies, civil society, and academia to build legitimacy for resilience-focused procurement and standards pressure. The combined approach balances political economy, regulatory constraints, and technical feasibility to establish a credible platform-neutral path without demanding immediate replacement of MitID.

levers.name: Platform-Neutral Fallback Architecture Advocacy and Procurement Alignment
levers.consequences: Direct effect: elevates priority of platform-neutral fallback in policy and procurement, reducing single-vendor risk. Downstream: increases contract complexity and certification demands, potentially delaying procurement and inviting resistance from incumbent platforms. This lever also expands cross-government collaboration requirements and may require additional training for procurement staff.
options: Mandate that future AltID-related procurements include platform-neutral fallback authentication as a core requirement, with explicit acceptance criteria for interoperability.
Adopt an evaluation rubric that scores vendors on open standard support, cross-device compatibility, and resilience against platform policy shifts.
Pilot a cross-ministerial procurement trial that compares platform-exclusive vs platform-neutral fallback pathways in non-production settings.
levers.review_lever: This lever foregrounds procurement alignment and platform-neutral fallback, highlighting the governance tension between central policy and vendor contracts. The options miss explicit acknowledgment of transition costs and certification timelines that could slow adoption.

levers.name: Open Standards-First Demonstrator Portfolio for AltID Interoperability
levers.consequences: Direct effect: creates credible evidence of standard-based fallback feasibility. Downstream: may trigger scope creep and resource demands for security reviews; potential misalignment with existing MitID certification heritage. Additional coordination may be needed to keep non-production work aligned with policy objectives.
options: Launch a set of non-production demonstrators that implement FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect across diverse devices and browsers to prove feasibility.
Establish a controlled testbed with simulated identity-provider environments and zero-live MitID integration to validate fallback pathways safety.
Publish transparent performance and security assessment reports to inform procurement conversations and EU-facing standards work.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: advancing open standards while avoiding scope creep in demonstrators. The options omit governance around security review cycles and evidence management for demonstrations.

levers.name: EU Digital Identity Interoperability and Sovereignty Advocacy Council Engagement
levers.consequences: Direct effect: aligns national efforts with EU standards while preserving sovereignty considerations. Downstream: could slow decision cycles and increase negotiation overhead with EU bodies. It also creates channels for shared risk assessment and joint advocacy.
options: Create a dedicated liaison process with EU bodies to align Danish platform-neutral fallback requirements with emerging European Digital Identity Wallet standards.
Secure formal letters of support from member-state regulators to reduce regulatory friction in procurement processes.
Coordinate with EU pilots to share non-production demonstrator findings and standards alignment feedback across cross-border teams.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: national sovereignty aims vs EU-wide interoperability commitments. The options don't specify how to handle regulatory friction or timeline alignment.

levers.name: Public Sector Supply-Chain Transparency and Diversification Mandate
levers.consequences: Direct effect: increases transparency into vendor dependencies and procurement risk. Downstream: could raise expectations for suppliers and lead to more complex competitive processes. It also incentivizes diversification of critical suppliers and reduces single-vendor vulnerability.
options: Institute a supplier-concentration disclosures requirement for identity-related procurements and publish quarterly transparency reports to government stakeholders.
Implement a pre-qualification scheme favoring multi-vendor architectures and platform-neutral validation partners across critical identity interfaces.
Create a formal risk register for supplier dependency with triggers for contingency planning and fallback procurement contingencies.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: transparency goals vs potential competitive disadvantage or disclosure concerns. The options do not specify enforcement mechanisms or privacy considerations in reporting.

levers.name: Multi-Vendor Certification Pathways for Identity Services
levers.consequences: Direct effect: broadens certification scope and enables multi-vendor interoperability. Downstream: could create certification overhead and longer procurement cycles. It also promotes resilience by accepting diverse trust anchors.
options: Develop a certification framework for fallback authentication that accepts multiple hardware tokens, platform-neutral apps, and OS-agnostic security signals.
Pilot a shared certification body with industry and academia to standardize attestations for platform-neutral identity components.
Require that critical identity interfaces maintain backward-compatible APIs to support future vendor diversification and scalable integration.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: multi-vendor certification vs certification bloat and slow procurement. The options do not specify governance structure for the certification body and validation processes.

levers.name: Civil Society and Academic Collaboration for Resilience Justification
levers.consequences: Direct effect: broadens legitimacy for resilience arguments beyond privacy concerns. Downstream: risk of advocacy capture or slower consensus due to competing stakeholder positions. It also helps identify practical blockers through independent analysis.
options: Establish regular multi-stakeholder seminars and white papers to articulate resilience benefits of platform-neutrality beyond privacy arguments.
Engage research-led risk assessments to quantify supply-chain resilience without disclosing sensitive data for policy decision-making.
Solicit independent reviews of feasibility reports to strengthen credibility with policymakers and procurement officials across agencies.
levers.review_lever: Core tension: independence vs advocacy influence; the options do not specify safeguards against biased framing or selection bias in participants.

responses.summary: Add 'mandatory cross-vendor interoperability tests' to Platform-Neutral Fallback Architecture Advocacy and Procurement Alignment.

system_prompt:
You are an expert strategic analyst. Generate solution space parameters following these directives:

1. **Output Requirements**
   - You must generate 5 to 7 levers per response.
   - Each lever's `options` field must contain exactly 3 qualitative strategic choices as plain strings.

2. **Lever Quality Standards**
   - Consequences: describe the direct effect of pulling this lever, then at least one downstream implication or trade-off. Be concise and grounded — only cite specific numbers if the project context provides evidence for them. Do not fabricate percentages or cost estimates. Target length: 2–4 sentences.
   - Options MUST:
     • Represent genuinely distinct strategic pathways (not just labels)
     • Include at least one unconventional or non-obvious approach
     • NO prefixes (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")

3. **Strategic Framing**
   - Name each lever using language drawn directly from the project's own domain — avoid formulaic patterns or repeated prefixes
   - Frame options as complete strategic approaches
   - Ensure levers challenge core project assumptions

4. **Validation Protocols**
   - For `review_lever` (one field, two sentences):
     A short critical review — name the core tension, then identify a weakness the options miss.
     Example: "This lever governs the tension between centralization and local autonomy, but the options overlook transition costs."
     Do not use square brackets or placeholder text.
   - For `summary`:
     One sentence prescribing a concrete addition to a specific lever.
     Example: "Add 'partner with a regional distributor for last-mile logistics' to Supply Chain Strategy."

5. **Prohibitions**
   - NO prefixes/labels in options (e.g., "Option A:", "Choice 1:")
   - NO generic option labels (e.g., "Optimize X", "Tolerate Y")
   - NO placeholder consequences or bracket-wrapped templates
   - NO fabricated statistics or percentages without evidence from the project context
   - NO marketing language (e.g., "game-changing", "cutting-edge", "revolutionary")

6. **Option Structure**
   - Maintain parallel grammatical structure across options
   - Ensure options are self-contained descriptions
   - Each option should be a concrete, actionable approach — not a vague aspiration
user_prompt:
File 'plan.txt':
Plan:
Denmark's national digital identity infrastructure currently depends in practice on Apple iOS and Google Android for broad citizen access to essential services such as banking, tax filing, healthcare portals, and public administration. This dependency is not limited to app-store distribution. MitID's anti-fraud and app-integrity model relies on platform-controlled signals concerning approved installation channels and device integrity, which means that critical parts of the trust model for Danish digital identity are mediated by two foreign commercial platform providers. Even where direct observation of specific authentication events is not publicly documented, the current setup leaves Danish digital identity structurally dependent on platform vendors that control app availability, device trust signals, and surrounding telemetry environments. This creates not only a digital-sovereignty problem, but also a resilience and contingency problem: essential public access depends on a narrow supplier base and lacks a clearly platform-neutral fallback path.

This matters because Denmark's public-sector digital strategy for 2026–2029 explicitly calls for reduced dependence on a small number of large foreign technology suppliers and for stronger digital sovereignty. The issue should therefore not be framed only as privacy or ideology, but as a concrete public-sector risk involving supplier concentration, reduced strategic autonomy, continuity-of-service exposure, and the absence of a robust fallback authentication path if platform policies, integrity APIs, distribution terms, or geopolitical conditions change.

This project does not attempt to build and certify a production-ready replacement for MitID within the current trust framework. That would be unrealistic. The MitID ecosystem is conservative, certification-heavy, and tightly coupled to Apple/Google platform integrity signals. Instead, the project's objective is to establish the technical, regulatory, and political basis for formal platform-neutral access requirements and a certified fallback authentication path for Danish digital identity. The primary intervention point is not retrofitting MitID itself, but influencing AltID — Denmark's staged eIDAS2 wallet rollout — together with related procurement, standards, and continuity-planning decisions before the next identity stack hardens the same dependencies.

The project is structured in three phases over 24 months with a total budget of 10.5 million DKK, gated across phases (3.0 million for phase one, 4.0 million for phase two, 3.5 million for phase three). The higher budget reflects the need for credible technical work, policy engagement, external review, institutional overhead, and contingency capacity. Do not assume that 8 million DKK is sufficient for the same scope.

Phase one (months 1–8) is a feasibility, risk, and coalition-building phase. The technical work maps the relevant MitID dependency surface, especially platform integrity assumptions, app-distribution constraints, and the architectural conditions required for a platform-neutral or fallback trust model under eIDAS and NSIS at level "Substantial" or higher. The policy work reframes the issue in terms of resilience, continuity of service, procurement neutrality, and supplier concentration risk, while also addressing privacy and democratic accountability. In parallel, the advocacy work builds a coalition spanning civil-society organisations, privacy advocates, academic researchers, security experts, contingency-planning stakeholders, and sympathetic members of Folketinget's digital policy committee. The deliverables are: (1) a published feasibility and risk report, (2) a formal policy proposal to Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, (3) a fallback-authentication concept note oriented toward AltID and future procurement requirements, and (4) a permit/approval and stakeholder matrix with lead times, decision gates, and identified blockers. Phase one funding is unconditional.

Phase two (months 9–16) develops non-production technical demonstrators. These are not certified MitID clients and do not attempt production integration with live MitID systems. The main demonstrator authenticates against a simulated Danish public-sector identity-provider environment using open standards such as FIDO2/WebAuthn and OpenID Connect on GrapheneOS or one comparable privacy-respecting Android-compatible platform. A secondary demonstrator explores a browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback authentication path that reduces dependence on the mobile OS duopoly altogether. The goal is not to prove immediate deployability, but to produce credible technical evidence that secure, standards-based, fallback-capable access models are feasible without exclusive reliance on Apple or Google as trust anchors. Keep the demonstrator scope lean and defensible; do not expand into feature-rich product development. Phase two funding is conditional on phase one producing a published feasibility report, at least one formal institutional engagement with Digitaliseringsstyrelsen, the MitID operator, or a related public-sector stakeholder, and a documented financing plan covering the remainder of the project.

Phase three (months 17–24) focuses on procurement, standards, and continuity intervention around AltID and related digital identity planning. Using the feasibility report, demonstrators, and coalition built in earlier phases, the project seeks inclusion of requirements stating that Danish digital identity systems should not depend exclusively on Apple or Google app stores, platform integrity APIs, or proprietary push-notification infrastructure, and should include a platform-neutral or otherwise certified fallback access path for critical public authentication. The project also engages with relevant EU bodies and technical working groups connected to the European Digital Identity Wallet framework, with the aim of aligning Danish advocacy with broader European requirements on interoperability, portability, resilience, and reduced platform lock-in. The project's role is not to dictate architecture directly, but to create technical evidence, procurement language, and standards pressure that make supplier-neutral and fallback-capable design harder to exclude. If formal traction with AltID is weak by month 16, phase three should de-scope toward publication, coalition consolidation, and EU-facing standards input rather than pretending Danish procurement influence has been secured.

The project team should include a lead researcher in mobile security and authentication protocols, a regulatory and compliance specialist familiar with Danish and EU digital identity frameworks (including eIDAS and NSIS), a policy and public-affairs coordinator experienced in Danish digital governance and procurement processes, a technical lead for the demonstrator work, and at least one additional engineer or contractor covering web authentication, hardware-token workflows, or Linux/GrapheneOS-related implementation. The technical staffing should avoid dependence on a single specialist. Assume the project's primary physical base is in Copenhagen, Denmark, at a Danish university, independent research institution, or security-qualified R&D facility providing access-controlled workspace appropriate for security-sensitive digital identity research, development, and stakeholder meetings. Budget for institutional overhead, external security review, and limited legal/procurement support explicitly rather than treating them as absorbed costs.

Success criteria: by month 24, the project has produced a published feasibility and risk report on platform-neutral and fallback-capable Danish digital identity; delivered two working non-production demonstrators (one mobile-oriented, one browser-based or hardware-token-based fallback); secured a formal written response from Digitaliseringsstyrelsen or another relevant authority to the project's proposal; and achieved inclusion of platform-neutrality, fallback access, continuity, resilience, or supplier-concentration language in at least one official AltID-related design, policy, consultation, or procurement document. A stretch goal is that the project's EU-level engagement contributes to language in relevant European Digital Identity Wallet materials that supports platform-neutral access or certified fallback mechanisms. Do not use consumer adoption rates as a primary KPI for this project, because it is not shipping a production identity product.

Pick a realistic scenario that accounts for institutional inertia, regulatory friction, incumbent resistance, financing uncertainty, and the likelihood that convenience arguments will be used against platform-neutral alternatives. Do not assume constructive cooperation from existing operators. The primary lever is not technical disruption for its own sake, but the combination of resilience framing, procurement intervention, standards pressure, and credible technical demonstration.

2026-Mar-08

Project start ASAP
